notes.note: M. Petreius and L. Afranius were Pompey's two legates commanding the Pompeian army in Spain; their fate against Caesar was the campaign whose outcome Cicero kept waiting for. The (false) report that they had united raised hopes that Caesar might be checked there.

notes.anchor: Pompey had marched with great forces into Germany by way of Illyricum
notes.note: A garbled rumour, reported "on first authority" yet quite wrong: Pompey had crossed the Adriatic to Greece. The conflicting bulletins reaching Cicero at Cumae show how blind he was to the war's real movements.

notes.anchor: So let us make for Malta, I think, until something happens in Spain
notes.note: Malta remains Cicero's chosen neutral bolt-hole, where he means to wait out the Spanish campaign before committing. The whole letter turns on his paralysis between Caesar's offered safety and loyalty to Pompey.

notes.anchor: The tears of my own people sometimes soften me
notes.note: Cicero's household --- Terentia, Tullia, and the two boys --- pressed him to stay in Italy and not throw himself and them away by joining Pompey. Their grief, and a piteous letter from Caelius, are set against his own resolve to act for his "standing" (dignitas).

notes.anchor: I am expecting Servius by the Nones
notes.note: Servius Sulpicius Rufus, the eminent jurist and consul of 51 BC, was another waverer Cicero hoped to consult; his wife Postumia and his son ("young Servius") were urging the meeting. The Nones of May fell on the 7th.
